metadata.raw_response_text:
:::writing
By 8:15 a.m., Elena Ruiz has already scanned 47 unread emails, two police reports, and a text from a client who spent the night in county jail wondering if anyone remembered him. Ruiz, a public defender in the midsize city of Riverton, sets her coffee down beside a stack of files thick enough to bow the metal shelf above her desk. She has 112 open cases. Today she will appear in court for nine of them.

This is not an unusual morning.

Riverton’s public defender office sits on the fourth floor of a concrete municipal building across from the courthouse. The hallway smells faintly of copier toner and burnt coffee. Ruiz’s office is barely wider than her desk. Case files are stacked in uneven towers: assault, probation violations, drug possession, retail theft, domestic disputes. She flips through them quickly, leaving sticky notes like breadcrumbs.

Most of her clients met her for the first time in a holding cell.

“People imagine big speeches in court,” Ruiz says. “Mostly it’s triage.”

Ruiz is 36, with a calm voice that carries easily in noisy courtrooms. She grew up in a nearby town where her mother worked nights as a nurse. After law school she briefly considered private practice but took a public defender job instead. “I liked the idea that the Constitution meant something real,” she says.

The reality, she admits, is messier.

Her morning docket begins with arraignments. Defendants shuffle forward in county-issued clothes while Ruiz stands beside them, whispering quick explanations: the charge, the bail amount, what happens next. Some are frightened. Some are angry. Some are exhausted in the flat way of people who have spent a night under fluorescent lights.

Between hearings she scribbles notes: call investigator, request body-cam footage, track down pharmacy records for a client claiming a prescription mix-up.

Every case contains a small universe of details that rarely make it into the official record. One client lost his job after missing a shift during arrest. Another is caring for a disabled parent. A third insists the police searched the wrong car.

“The law is one story,” Ruiz says. “People’s lives are another.”

By lunchtime she has negotiated two plea deals, scheduled three trials, and convinced a judge to lower bail for a man accused of shoplifting diapers and formula. She eats a granola bar at her desk while listening to voicemail. One caller cries. Another hangs up mid-sentence.

Heavy caseloads are the quiet backdrop of public defense nationwide. Professional guidelines recommend far fewer cases per attorney than Ruiz currently carries. In Riverton, budget debates often circle back to the same question: how much defense is enough.

Ruiz shrugs at the numbers. “If someone’s freedom is on the line, that case matters to them more than anything in the world,” she says. “So you try to treat it that way.”

In the afternoon she meets a new client in a small interview room at the jail. The man sits across from her in an orange uniform, hands folded, trying to explain what happened during a chaotic night outside a bar. Ruiz listens carefully, occasionally stopping him to clarify timelines or names.

By the end of the meeting she has a rough plan: subpoena security footage, interview a bartender, check whether the arresting officer’s report matches the timeline.

It is nearly 6 p.m. when Ruiz returns to the office. The courthouse has emptied, but her email has not. She opens another file, reads a police narrative, and begins drafting a motion to suppress evidence.

Tomorrow morning the stack on her desk will still be there. There will also be new files, new clients, new urgent messages.

Before shutting down her computer, Ruiz sends a short reply to the man who texted from jail the night before.

“I’m your lawyer,” it reads. “I’m working on your case.”
:::
